ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general congee class (FOODON_03301710). No specific entry for minced pork with century egg congee exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers rice porridge/congee as a category. No specific minced pork century egg congee entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific congee preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of minced pork with century egg congee, approximately 400 g, as typically served in a Singaporean coffeeshop or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Congee (Bubur Babi) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists pork congee (bubur babi) as the closest approved match. Minced pork with century egg congee is a common variant; values are adapted from HPB pork congee data. Century egg adds modest protein and fat; overall macro profile is consistent with HPB pork congee reference. Per-100g values are derived from HPB data for pork congee (approximately 70 kcal/100g, protein 4.5g, fat 1.8g, carbohydrate 9.5g) with minor upward adjustment for century egg contribution. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and seasoning typical of this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice porridge / congee
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for minced pork with century egg congee. Rice congee/porridge has a published GI of approximately 57 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). The addition of minced pork and century egg lowers the effective GI slightly due to protein and fat content, but the base GI of 57 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving calculated as (57 × 9.2 × 4.0) / 100 = 21.0, rounded to 21.1 for a 400g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 400g serving provides approximately 1280 mg sodium, which represents over 55% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index rice congee base with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 57 and a glycemic load of ~21 per standard 400g serving, this dish causes a moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from pork and century egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 55 mg cholesterol per 100g and high sodium (~320 mg/100g), both of which are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose lower-sodium preparation methods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Minced Pork with Century Egg Congee (肉末皮蛋粥, Rou Mo Pi Dan Zhou) is a classic Singaporean Chinese rice porridge dish consisting of a smooth, slow-cooked rice congee base topped or mixed with seasoned minced pork and sliced century egg (皮蛋, pi dan). Century egg is a preserved duck or chicken egg with a dark, gelatinous white and a creamy, strongly flavoured yolk, produced through an alkaline curing process. The congee is simmered until the rice breaks down into a thick, silky consistency, then seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, white pepper, and ginger. It is typically garnished with spring onions and fried shallots. The dish is savoury, umami-rich, and comforting, with a soft texture that makes it suitable for all ages. It is commonly eaten as breakfast, a light meal, or a recovery food when unwell. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~72 kcal/100g) with a reasonable protein content (~4.8g/100g) from the pork and egg, low fat, and a medium glycemic index (~57) due to the well-cooked rice base. The main clinical concern is its elevated sodium content from seasoning, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content.

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo provides a pork congee reference that is the closest approved primary source match. No exact entry for minced pork with century egg congee was found; values are adapted from the HPB pork congee entry with minor adjustments for century egg contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice congee. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo pork congee source entry. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo pork congee source entry. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo pork congee source entry. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork and egg component data as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this dish; trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for this preparation). The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Meat and alternatives'; this has been reclassified to 'Grains and staples' as congee is a rice-based dish where the grain is the primary component. The field dataed (Viet'Spice) serves Vietnamese-influenced cuisine but this dish is a standard Singaporean Chinese congee preparation. Price data (SGD 12.90) is preserved from the input record for reference only and is not used in nutritional analysis. GI category is medium (GI = 57, which is 56–69 range per Veritas threshold rules).
